name: Archie, Heard
motto: "He is a man that does his own thinking."
description: At a first introduction to Archie, one would have taken him as being possessed with the sobriety and solemnity supposed to adhere to all Seniors, but when the real person was known and all work was finished, he was the life of the situation and could originate more jokes than any one. Combining his good common sense with his fun-making talent, he was one of the best and most successful students of the Magnolia Aggie School. He was one of the most loyal of the loyal members of his society. He is a graduate of the A. T. R. C., was elected president of the Garland Literary Society 1913-14-15. Was sent as representative of the Y. M. C. A. to the Student Volunteer Convention held at Arkadelphia, 1914. As an athlete he was conferred the honor of manager of the basketball team, 1913-14. He proved his oratory in the Magnolia-Monticello debate by being winner, 1915. In all affairs, social or religious, he was an ardent solicitor.
text:
"He is a man that does his own thinking."

name: Annie, Cooper
description: Annie is a graduate of the M. H. S., 1911. At this institution she proved herself to be a very studious personage. She was valedictorian of her class. She entered the Aggie school in 1913, and although she dropped out twice to play "school keeping," she carried on her work with success and honor to the end. She was a faithful member of the Garland Literary Society and also of the Country Club.
text:
"Brilliancy is her name."
Annie is a graduate of the M. H. S., 1911. At this institution she proved herself to be a very studious personage. She was valedictorian of her class. She entered the Aggie school in 1913, and although she dropped out twice to play "school keeping," she carried on her work with success and honor to the end. She was a faithful member of the Garland Literary Society and also of the Country Club.

name: Fuller, Bond
motto: "The mildness of his inner self is readily recognized by all, through his calm, placid countenance."
clubs: Y. M. C. A. (delegate to state convention, 1913)
description: At the beginning of the 1912-13 session, Fuller strolled unobtrusively into the T. D. A. S. He entered as a Sophomore and despite his quiet, unassuming nature his schoolmates always knew when he was around. He was a very loyal member of the Yuma Society, and held the honorable offices of vice president in 1913, and president in 1914-15. He was a diligent worker for the Y. M. C. A. and was sent as a delegate to the state convention in 1913. He was vice president of the Progressive Agricultural Club, 1913-14. He proved his worth as a writer by taking the first honor for an Agricultural "write-up," thus winning for himself the position of Agricultural editor on the Monitor staff. He was also an active member of the Country Club. Through his influence rural communities will be made better, and their serious problems solved.

name: Myrtle, Finney
description: Myrtle was an ex-Russellville Aggie and notwithstanding the fact that she would not say anything disrespectful against her former Aggie home, it was very noticeable that she was partial to her latter, which she entered in 1913. She was a true Garland; also a member of the Country Club. In her Home Economics she had no equal. Better homes will be made through her influence.
text:
"Modesty and serenity are her characteristics."
Myrtle was an ex-Russellville Aggie and notwithstanding the fact that she would not say anything disrespectful against her former Aggie home, it was very noticeable that she was partial to her latter, which she entered in 1913.
